content:
Facts, Sources, and Fallacies STATING FACTS AND CITING SOURCES This week’s assignment is two parts: First I will be looking at a historical assertion from one of my sources and then I will examine that assertion in two other historical works, looking closely at how other historians interpret that assertion.
I looked at the assertion Richard Rothstein makes in his book The Color of Law: A Forgotten History of How Our Government Segregated America, in which Rothstein argues that segregation in the United States was intentional, occurred by government action and therefore is de facto and not de jure. Rothstein says that such segregation is by law and public policy and not by personal decision or choice. 1 To check this claim, I searched through James Kushner’s foundational work Apartheid in America: An Historical and Legal Analysis of Contemporary Racial Residential Segregation in the United States, which reinforces the claim that America’s segregation was de facto instead of de jure. Rothstein based much of his argument on Kushner’s legal journal article. Cross-checking Rothstein’s citation also allowed me to see Kushner’s argument in its original context, which also helped me to gain a better grasp of de facto and de jure segregation.
The second part of this assignment concerned reading the introduction of David Hackett Fischer’s Historians’ Fallacies: Toward a Logic of Historical Thought and familiarizing myself with Fischer’s three categories of fallacies and their respective chapters. I then chose one of Fischer’s fallacies to apply to a historical source from my research.
Fischer defines a historical fallacy as errors in historical scholarship that lead to false assumptions and procedures. He specifically notes that “a fallacy is not merely an error in and of itself, but a way of falling into error."1 and breaks such fallacies into three categories: fallacies of Inquiry, fallacies of explanation, and fallacies of argument. For my assignment, I chose to look at James Lowen’s 2009 book Sundown Towns, which makes the sweeping claim that the southern United States escaped Sundown Towns that plagued the Northern and Western portions of the U.S., through the lens of Fischer’s fallacy called the overwhelming exception. This fallacy makes sweeping generalizations that lead the reader to falsely believe something to be true. For example, Loewen claims that Sundown towns were not common in the South. By making this argument, he fails to note that Southern states were not only home to Sundown towns and counties, Forsyth County in Georgia is a notable example, but he also leads the reader to create a false assumption that perhaps racial issues in Southern towns were not as bad as their Northern counterparts.
summary:
Facts, Sources, and Fallacies

STATING FACTS AND CITING SOURCES

Rothstein says that such segregation is by law and public policy and not by personal decision or choice. 1 To check this claim, I searched through James Kushner’s foundational work Apartheid in America: An Historical and Legal Analysis of Contemporary Racial Residential Segregation in the United States, which reinforces the claim that America’s segregation was de facto instead of de jure. Rothstein based much of his argument on Kushner’s legal journal article. Cross-checking Rothstein’s citation also allowed me to see Kushner’s argument in its original context, which also helped me to gain a better grasp of de facto and de jure segregation.
title: Facts, Sources, and Fallacies

content:
Spinning Time Timelines can be useful tools for visualizing how historical events unfolded. The JS Timeline tool from Knightlab allows the user to create digital timelines and publish them on the web. On this post I record a few thoughts regarding creating a digital timeline with the JS Timeline tool.
I chose to create a timeline about residential segregation ordinances in twentieth century America. I am addressing this topic in my thesis and already have sources complied. JS Timeline creators suggest limiting your timeline to twenty events or less. My current published timeline has ten events, but I may add more later.
To get started, I copied the spreadsheet that JS Timeline provided and opened my copy in Google docs. The spreadsheet is already tabulated to insert data into the timeline, all I have to do is plug my data into the appropriate row and column, click publish to the web, and copy/paste the link to the spreadsheet into the timeline generator. After this is done, I can embed my timeline into a website, either via a URL or shortcode.
The process sounds easy. I will say that, for the most part, it was easy, except for occasional glitches that occur. For instance, I had a difficult time inserting images into my timeline. Any image or multimedia must have a URL associated with it in order to be inserted into the Timeline spreadsheet. Photos that I have taken myself gave me the most difficulty since they were not from a website. To work around this issue, I uploaded these images to my website in a post and then inserted that URL into the timeline spreadsheet. There is most likely a better way to deal with this issue, but for now, it works.
I find timelines to be valuable digital tools in that they offer a quick way to communicate the basics about a historical event in a manner that can be easily understood. In a sense, they are a very “public-facing” tool. Timelines are known for brevity; too many words will visually bog them down. If you want an audience to have quick exposure to concepts or events, including how those events unfolded, then a timeline might be one of the best visual tools that can be used.
summary:
Spinning Time

Timelines can be useful tools for visualizing how historical events unfolded. The JS Timeline tool from Knightlab allows the user to create digital timelines and publish them on the web. On this post I record a few thoughts regarding creating a digital timeline with the JS Timeline tool.

I chose to create a timeline about residential segregation ordinances in twentieth century America. I am addressing this topic in my thesis and already have sources complied. JS Timeline creators suggest limiting your timeline to twenty events or less. My current published timeline has ten events, but I may add more later.
title: Spinning Time

content:
Time in the Archive This week, I visited the Clemson University Special Collections and Archives to get hands-on archival experience. Before I visited the archive, my class was given a list of primary source materials to choose a topic. I chose The James F. Byrnes papers. I knew a little about Byrnes, as he had popped up in my research, but I needed to know more about him. I did a quick Google search to help me choose what possible topics from Byrnes’s life might be interesting to me. I used the finding aides on the Special Collections website to assist me in narrowing down my selections from the 3,000 plus items that are included in Byrne’s papers. I decided to focus on Byrnes’s sixteen-month stent as a justice of the United States Supreme Court, which occurred during World War II. This time period is confined to sources from Series 3 of the collection. I then requested Boxes 1, 2, and 6. These boxes contained various folders with mixed materials.
I also found that I could use the finding aids to get a better understanding of what materials are inside of this box. The screenshot below displays the box’s folders and a description of what each folder might contain.
I opened box six first and started going through each folder, one at a time, while taking notes on anything that caught my attention. Byrnes had spent many years as a U.S. Senator for South Carolina before his appointment to the U.S. Supreme Court. This box contained correspondence from his former constituents who still wanted his help in matters back in South Carolina. Each letter that was written to Byrnes had an attached copy of his response, stating that he could not offer assistance due to his position on the Supreme Court. A few of these letters caught my attention.
In box 6, folder 1, which is labeled as Professional Correspondence, I found this letter from the Royal Cola Company, also known as RC Cola, asking for assistance in obtaining more sugar to make their colas. The cola company noted that due to sugar rations imposed by the government to help with the war efforts, they could not meet the demands that had been placed on them, specifically noting that they supply cola to soldiers. A letter such as this, could be helpful for historical researchers who research the economic impact of war upon local private companies. How did rationing create issues with supply and demand in local economies?
As I worked, I took pictures of things that interested me. I uploaded them to Tropy and entered metadata for each item so that I could construct a bibliography to use later. Below is a screenshot showing the letter from RC Cola in Tropy, along with its metadata.
As I mentioned earlier, South Carolinians continued to write to Byrnes for assistance after he was appointed to the Supreme Court. Many of these letters survived and were tucked into several of the folders that I came across in my research. These letters often were from parents of soldiers who were writing to Byrnes on behalf of their “boys,” asking for better provisions in the field. Although I grew up surrounded by World War II veterans, I was not aware of the shortages that crippled the armed forces until I read these letters. Byrnes eventually stepped down from the Supreme Court to assist the Roosevelt administration in the war effort, specifically working on how to ensure supply and demand were accomplished. These letters would make an interesting research project into how the public moved government officials to become better stewards of materials in order to further the war effort.
A letter asking Justice Byrnes to help “our Hartsville boys”
Byrnes and President Franklin D. Roosevelt shared a friendship that was demonstrated in their correspondence. The Telegraph below shows Byrnes wishing the President Happy Birthday, and the memo contains a joke about both of them being rebuffed by the State Department. Items such as these personal notes help to bring a fullness to historical actors and also can explain why someone like Byrnes might step down from a life appointment to the Supreme Court to work in the Roosevelt administration. Not only had Byrnes had a first-hand glimpse of wartime shortages and their effects on soldiers from the many letters that he had received from his parents, but also his welcoming of an administration job allowed him to work closely with Roosevelt as a trusted friend and ally.
This was a fun exercise and provided me with some valuable time understanding how an archive works.
summary:
Time in the Archive

This week, I visited the Clemson University Special Collections and Archives to get hands-on archival experience. Before I visited the archive, my class was given a list of primary source materials to choose a topic. I chose The James F. Byrnes papers. I knew a little about Byrnes, as he had popped up in my research, but I needed to know more about him. I did a quick Google search to help me choose what possible topics from Byrnes’s life might be interesting to me. I used the finding aides on the Special Collections website to assist me in narrowing down my selections from the 3,000 plus items that are included in Byrne’s papers. I decided to focus on Byrnes’s sixteen-month stent as a justice of the United States Supreme Court, which occurred during World War II. This time period is confined to sources from Series 3 of the collection. I then requested Boxes 1, 2, and 6. These boxes contained various folders with mixed materials.
title: Time in the Archive

content:
Current writing and research projects:
Co-authoring upcoming book with Dr. Abel Bartley on the history of Springfield Baptist Church in Greenville, South Carolina
Co-authoring book chapter about the significance of Springfield Baptist Church in The New Greenbook of South Carolina with Dr. Andrew Baker.
Ongoing work on notable Black citizens in the history of Greenville, South Carolina with emphasis on the years of 1877 to 1941. This work includes creating a new database, conducting a network analysis of findings, and using GIS to model where significant citizens lived and worked.

content:
Historical Silences and the Struggle to Remember A few years ago, my kids and I visited Monticello. After touring the main floor of the house and marveling at the many curiosities that it contained, we made our way to the basement, where enslaved persons toiled to make the beautiful environment above the stairs possible. We sat in the room that historians believed to belong to Sally Hemmings and watched the digital presentation of her life. We toured the reconstructed cabins that housed many of those enslaved on the plantation. All of this was moving and meaningful, but there was one moment on the “slave tour” that stopped me in my tracks. The tour guide mentioned that historians working on the plantation were struggling to identify and tell the stories of field slaves. These enslaved persons were said to live on the edges of Jefferson’s fields in temporary housing and were required to shift that housing according to what field demanded their attention.
The struggle for modern-day historians and archeologists was that there was not much left behind regarding these people. The idea that I cannot tell someone’s story because there is nothing left of them to tell, aside from purchase orders in a ledger and perhaps a notation of their death, profoundly struck me. The fabric of these people’s lives mattered; their stories speak to the injustice of slavery that fueled our country. And yet, they exist as ghosts in the narrative of American history. That day sparked an interest in uncovering historical silences in America’s history and doing my best to assist in helping a nation that is far too comfortable forgetting, to remember.
In the past few years, Clemson University has been on a journey to uncover and remember enslaved persons who lived on the plantation where the University was established. If you have been to a football game at Death Valley, you have probably noticed that the stadium shares a hill with a cemetery. On this hill lies the family graves of those who originally owned the plantation, as well as many former University Presidents and faculty, as well as hundreds of unmarked graves of formerly enslaved people, convicts who were leased to build the University, and many others. The stories of these people have largely gone untold until recently. Advancements in ground penetrating technology have enabled the University to identify these graves and commemorate them with a granite marker. Last March, I toured the graveyard after the grave markers were placed and was moved by the overwhelming effect of so many markers. Each one is an effort to recall, remember, and honor someone who lived, breathed, and died. They are simple markers, painfully simple, but powerful in stating that these people mattered.
summary:
Historical Silences and the Struggle to Remember

A few years ago, my kids and I visited Monticello. After touring the main floor of the house and marveling at the many curiosities that it contained, we made our way to the basement, where enslaved persons toiled to make the beautiful environment above the stairs possible. We sat in the room that historians believed to belong to Sally Hemmings and watched the digital presentation of her life. We toured the reconstructed cabins that housed many of those enslaved on the plantation. All of this was moving and meaningful, but there was one moment on the “slave tour” that stopped me in my tracks. The tour guide mentioned that historians working on the plantation were struggling to identify and tell the stories of field slaves. These enslaved persons were said to live on the edges of Jefferson’s fields in temporary housing and were required to shift that housing according to what field demanded their attention.
title: Historical Silences and the Struggle to Remember

content:
Text Analysis with Voyant Tools This week, I am exploring how to use Voyant tools in text analysis. Voyant Tools provides web-based reading and visualization tools for digital text.
I chose three tools to try out from the many that Voyant offers. First, I learned about the Topics tool. This tool assists researchers in understanding what topics or term clusters exist in a corpus of work. The tool sorts the words in the corpus by randomly assigning each word a number that corresponds to a topic. The occurrences of these topics, as determined by the number of term clusters that have been returned for the topic, are then visualized by the voyant tool.
I also experimented with the Corpus Collocates tool, which can be used to view words that appear more frequently within a corpus and the proximity that these words have to keywords in the text. These words are broken down into three parts: the term (the keyword), the collocate (those words found in close proximity to the keyword), and the count (the number of instances in which the term and collocate are found in close proximity within the corpus). The Collocates Graph tool works similarly to the Corpus Collocates tool but presents the terms and collocates in a visualization that resembles a network.
While Voyant claims that their suite of tools allows users to “see through the text,” I think they reveal relationships and interconnectedness within the text. This could be useful in assessing primary sources to see what topics are connected within their corpus.
summary:
Text Analysis with Voyant Tools

This week, I am exploring how to use Voyant tools in text analysis. Voyant Tools provides web-based reading and visualization tools for digital text.

I chose three tools to try out from the many that Voyant offers. First, I learned about the Topics tool. This tool assists researchers in understanding what topics or term clusters exist in a corpus of work. The tool sorts the words in the corpus by randomly assigning each word a number that corresponds to a topic. The occurrences of these topics, as determined by the number of term clusters that have been returned for the topic, are then visualized by the voyant tool.
title: Text Analysis with Voyant Tools

content:
What is Digital History? What is digital history? This question is one that I hear constantly. I have recently begun to ask the questioner what it is they might think before I attempt to answer myself. A few of the answers: History with a computer? History that uses spreadsheets? And, my all-time personal favorite, a worldwide conspiracy for “them” to control us all by placing all history on servers controlled by the elite.
But all kidding aside, digital history is an approach to doing history by imploring digital tools. It involves traditional and digital research methods to explore historical topics, trends, and relationships that cannot be discovered without the use of digital tools. Digital history is a broad and diverse field that rewards curious historians with the ability to see history in new ways.
This post will look closely at three different examples of digital scholarship. I will be examining what identifiers make these research projects uniquely digital. What data sets are used? What digital tools and methods have been wielded upon those datasets? And who is behind these projects.
The first example of digital scholarship that I looked at was the website Gilded Age Plains City, which tells the story of urbanization in the Midwest through the lens of a famous murder trial in the 1890s. The project grew from a 2001 article by Timothy R. Mahoney entitled history profess The Great Sheedy Murder Case and the Booster Ethos of the Gilded Age in Lincoln, Nebraska. The project was headed by Mahoney, who worked with students at the Center for Digital Research in the Humanities at the University of Nebraska. This project used the exploration of spatial narratives to provide a deeper understanding of the world in which this famous trial took place. The spatial narratives are combined with an interactive map of Lincoln, Nebraska. Viewers are able to hover over buildings and discover who their inhabitants are, their relevance to the case, and the scope of what the city is like at this moment in time. The creators of this project believe that by imploring the use of spatial narratives, they are able to provide a much fuller experience of their historical topic than traditional analog methods. The website developers also placed their digitized sources in an online archive, which is easy to navigate for the user.
summary:
What is Digital History?

What is digital history? This question is one that I hear constantly. I have recently begun to ask the questioner what it is they might think before I attempt to answer myself. A few of the answers: History with a computer? History that uses spreadsheets? And, my all-time personal favorite, a worldwide conspiracy for “them” to control us all by placing all history on servers controlled by the elite.
title: What is Digital History

content:
Net.Create is an open-source analysis tool that allows for the simultaneous building of a network by multiple users. The ability to create a network simultaneously with other researchers gives Net.Create the ability to foster creativity and responsiveness within a research team. Using metadata from the reading selections that we have worked on this semester, our digital history class used Net.Create to craft a network that represented the interconnected nature of these readings.
First, we made note of the metadata for each of our assigned readings. This included not only a bibliographic citation and DOI, but also possibly a summary or abstract of the reading. The information was then used to construct nodes and edges in Net.Create. Our nodes were the authors of our assigned readings, and the edges were the interconnectivity between theses authors. Perhaps they were connected by joint authorship or subject. The end result was a visual reminder that many of our readings overlapped areas of scholarship and were more connected to each other than we realized.
Here is a screenshot of our Nodes table with metadata concerning authorship displayed.
Our edges table.
An example of the attributes that have been entered for an assigned reading.
A glimpse of the emerging network as it was being built simultaneously by students during class.
summary: Net.Create is an open-source analysis tool that allows for the simultaneous building of a network by multiple users. The ability to create a network simultaneously with other researchers gives Net.Create the ability to foster creativity and responsiveness within a research team. Using metadata from the reading selections that we have worked on this semester, our digital history class used Net.Create to craft a network that represented the interconnected nature of these readings.
title: Net.Create

content:
Dora Helen Impson “Posterity! You will never know how much it cost the present generation to preserve your freedom. I hope you will make good use of it.” These words from John Adams articulate why I have chosen to become a historian.
My love of history stems from my grandparents, who were part of the generation that endured World War II. They were my playmates and first history teachers, and I owe them an outstanding debt. My thirst for historical knowledge is rooted in the lessons I learned at my grandparents’ side. The importance of sharing this knowledge with future generations was impressed on me by those who truly understood the price that has been paid for freedom.
I am a Ph.D student studying Digital History at Clemson where my research interests include looking at the intersection of racial relations, religious beliefs, and civil rights in American History through the lens of Legal History. Topics of interest to me are: the Black experience in America in terms of property ownership and loss, lament and the American church’s responses to racial atrocities, and the use of Christianity as agency in American history.
I am interested in partnering with other digital historians to explore how to make history from the Reconstruction to modern times accessible, engaging, and inspiring for young people in the United States.
summary:
Dora Helen Impson

“Posterity! You will never know how much it cost the present generation to preserve your freedom. I hope you will make good use of it.” These words from John Adams articulate why I have chosen to become a historian.

My love of history stems from my grandparents, who were part of the generation that endured World War II. They were my playmates and first history teachers, and I owe them an outstanding debt. My thirst for historical knowledge is rooted in the lessons I learned at my grandparents’ side. The importance of sharing this knowledge with future generations was impressed on me by those who truly understood the price that has been paid for freedom.
title: About
